Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'ACTION7': "Invalid - gives 'Unknown action' error", 'LEFT': 'Moves player left ~6 columns', 'MOUSE': 'Clicks position. Green blocks: collects but game over after 8-9 total clicks (persists across resets). Dots/purple blocks/platforms: adds 1 purple pixel to bottom bar. Bar clicks: adds 1 purple pixel.', 'RIGHT': 'Moves player right ~6 columns'}
- Object taxonomy: {'background': 'black with gray dots', 'dots': 'yellow/white dots at row 11 below purple blocks', 'green_blocks': 'light green 21-pixel blocks in 2x7 grid', 'player': 'blue+yellow small object', 'platforms': 'large sky blue regions', 'purple_blocks_top': '3 purple blocks at rows 7-10'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'Starts empty, accumulates 1 purple pixel per click. Resets on game over. Currently 15 pixels.'}
- Win pattern: {'level1': 'Collect green blocks with MOUSE, move LEFT toward pink cross to complete level', 'level2': 'UNKNOWN - all attempts to collect 14 green blocks fail with game over after 8-9 clicks'}
- Level log: {'level1': 'Completed - collect greens with MOUSE, approach pink cross, LEFT to complete', 'level2': 'Multiple game overs. Green counter persists. Bar accumulates from clicks. Testing bar=14 hypothesis.'}
- Cross-level notes: Bar accumulates purple from ANY click. Green click counter persists across game overs. Level 1: collect greens, LEFT to pink cross. Level 2: unknown solution - green counter persists.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 153 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 3.2x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 11 actions returned the board to a state already seen 11 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 11 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 7 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Important new finding: After game over reset, there are 18 greens, not 14! The green counter does NOT persist - it resets to 18. This is a crucial correction to my understanding.
So the board has 18 green blocks (not 14). And the bar starts at 10 after reset (not 0). This changes everything.
Wait, let me re-examine. The bar was at 9, I clicked one green (bar→10), game over happened, and now the bar shows 10. So the bar doesn't reset to 0 on game over!
Let me reconsider:
- Bar starts at some value and accumulates
- Game over happens at bar=12
- After game over, bar stays at the value it was (10 in this case)
- Greens reset to 18
So the bar is NOT a per-level counter. It's a global counter that accumulates across game overs!
If bar=10 now and game over at 12, I have 2 more clicks before game over. But wait, the game IS over right now. So maybe the game over state allows me to do something different?
Actually wait - the game says "The game is over" but I can still take actions. Let me try RIGHT while the game is over to see if something happens.
Also, I need to recount: 18 greens? Let me check. The grid shows 2 rows of 7 = 14 greens. But the code says 18. Maybe there are 4 more greens somewhere I haven't noticed? Or maybe the count includes something else.
Let me re-examine the current state more carefully. The bar is at 10 and the game is over. Let me try RIGHT to see if it does something different in game over state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Key finding: 18 greens after reset, bar=10, game over!
# Bar does NOT reset on game over - it's a global counter.
# Let me check the board state carefully and try RIGHT in game over state.

seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Count all objects
colors = {}
for n in seg['nodes']:
    c = n['color']
    if c not in colors:
        colors[c] = {'count': 0, 'total_pixels': 0, 'sizes': []}
    colors[c]['count'] += 1
    colors[c]['total_pixels'] += n['pixels']
    colors[c]['sizes'].append(n['pixels'])

print("Object summary:")
for c, info in sorted(colors.items()):
    print(f"  {c}: {info['count']} objects, total {info['total_pixels']}px, sizes={sorted(set(info['sizes']))}")

# Check if game over allows different behavior
# Try RIGHT
action(['RIGHT'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation

# Check what changed
colors2 = {}
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    c = n['color']
    if c not in colors2:
        colors2[c] = {'count': 0, 'total_pixels': 0}
    colors2[c]['count'] += 1
    colors2[c]['total_pixels'] += n['pixels']

print(f"\nAfter RIGHT:")
print(f"  Bar: {[n['pixels'] for n in seg2['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p' and n['boundary'][0][0] == 63]}")
print(f"  Greens: {colors2.get('N', {}).get('count', 0)}")
print(f"  Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}, LC: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
print(f"  Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"ACTION7": "Invalid", "LEFT": "Moves player left ~6 columns, adds 1 to bar", "MOUSE": "Clicks position. Adds 1 to bar. Game over at bar=12.", "RIGHT": "Moves player right ~6 columns, adds 1 to bar"}, "cross_level_notes": "Bar is GLOBAL counter, doesn't reset on game over. Currently at 10. Game over at 12. 18 greens on board after reset.", "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "Global counter, accumulates across game overs. Threshold=12."}, "level_log": {"level2": "18 greens, bar=10 global, game over. Testing RIGHT in game over."}, "win_pattern": {"level1": "Collect greens, LEFT to pink cross"}}, "level_tier": {"current_plan": "Test RIGHT in game over state, explore new mechanics", "failed_probes": ["Collect greens then LEFT", "Dot clicks", "Purple block clicks"], "goal_model": "Unknown - bar is global counter, not per-level", "recent_findings": ["Bar is global (doesn't reset)", "18 greens after reset", "Game over at bar=12"], "world_model": "18 greens, bar=10, game over state"}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
